clause_playbook.text: Landlord may retain all or part of the security deposit for cleaning, repainting, carpet wear, general wear and tear, administrative costs, or any other condition landlord deems necessary.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: This lets the landlord charge the tenant for ordinary wear and vague administrative costs.
meta.market_standard: Security deposit deductions should be limited to unpaid rent, documented damages beyond ordinary wear, and itemized lawful charges.
meta.counter: Security deposit deductions must be limited to unpaid rent and itemized damage beyond ordinary wear and tear, with receipts provided within the legally required return window.
meta.demo_hook: The landlord can keep your deposit for normal wear.

clause_playbook.text: Security deposit will be returned after landlord completes inspection and accounting.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: There is no firm deadline, which can make it hard for the tenant to recover the deposit quickly.
meta.market_standard: The lease should identify the statutory return deadline or a specific number of days.
meta.counter: Security deposit must be returned with an itemized statement within the deadline required by applicable law.

clause_playbook.text: If rent is late, tenant will pay a late fee of $150 plus $25 per day until rent is paid in full.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: Daily compounding late fees can become punitive and disproportionate.
meta.market_standard: Late fees should have a short grace period and a reasonable cap.
meta.counter: Late fees apply only after a 5-day grace period and are capped at the lesser of $50 or the maximum allowed by law.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant is responsible for all repairs, replacements, maintenance, pest control, appliance failures, plumbing issues, electrical issues, and habitability-related expenses during the lease term.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: This shifts core landlord maintenance duties to the tenant.
meta.market_standard: Tenants handle minor damage they cause; landlords handle habitability, systems, appliances provided with the unit, and structural repairs.
meta.counter: Tenant is responsible only for damage caused by tenant or guests. Landlord remains responsible for habitability, building systems, included appliances, and structural repairs.
meta.demo_hook: Tenant pays for plumbing and electrical failures.

clause_playbook.text: Landlord may enter the premises at any time for inspection, repairs, showings, maintenance, emergencies, or any business purpose.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: This removes meaningful notice and privacy protections.
meta.market_standard: Entry should require reasonable written notice except for emergencies.
meta.counter: Landlord may enter only with at least 24 hours' notice during reasonable hours, except in emergencies or as otherwise required by law.
meta.demo_hook: Landlord can enter at any time.

clause_playbook.text: If tenant terminates early, tenant remains liable for all rent through the end of the lease term, regardless of whether landlord re-rents the premises.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: This ignores the landlord's duty to mitigate by trying to re-rent the unit.
meta.market_standard: Tenant may owe reasonable reletting costs and rent until the unit is re-rented, subject to mitigation.
meta.counter: If tenant terminates early, landlord must make commercially reasonable efforts to re-rent, and tenant liability ends when a replacement tenancy begins.
meta.demo_hook: You could pay rent after someone else moves in.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant may not assign, sublet, host, license, or otherwise permit any other person to occupy the premises for any period without landlord's sole and absolute discretion.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: Absolute discretion can block reasonable life changes or roommate replacements.
meta.market_standard: Landlord consent should not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed.
meta.counter: Tenant may request assignment or sublet approval, and landlord consent may not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed.

clause_playbook.text: Landlord may increase rent, fees, utility charges, or other amounts at any time upon written notice.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: A fixed-term lease should not allow unilateral price changes during the term.
meta.market_standard: Rent should be fixed during the lease term except for agreed pass-throughs clearly stated in the lease.
meta.counter: Rent and recurring fees are fixed during the lease term unless both parties agree in writing or a specific lawful pass-through is listed here.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant agrees to pay all utilities, shared charges, building charges, service fees, billing fees, and any other charges allocated by landlord.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: Open-ended fees make the real monthly cost unpredictable.
meta.market_standard: Utility responsibility and allocation formulas should be explicit.
meta.counter: Tenant is responsible only for utilities and fees specifically listed in this lease, with shared utilities allocated by a disclosed formula and supporting bills available on request.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant waives the right to bring or participate in any class action and agrees all disputes must be resolved by binding arbitration selected by landlord.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: This limits the tenant's ability to join claims with others and gives landlord control over the forum.
meta.market_standard: Dispute clauses should preserve statutory tenant rights and use a neutral forum.
meta.counter: Disputes may be resolved in any court or forum available under applicable law, and tenant does not waive non-waivable statutory rights.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant shall indemnify landlord from all claims, losses, damages, costs, and attorney fees arising from the premises, regardless of cause.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: The tenant could be liable for claims caused by the landlord or building defects.
meta.market_standard: Tenant indemnity should be limited to tenant's negligence, misconduct, or breach.
meta.counter: Tenant indemnity applies only to claims caused by tenant's negligence, willful misconduct, or breach, and excludes landlord negligence or building defects.

clause_playbook.text: This lease automatically renews for another 12 months unless tenant gives written notice at least 120 days before expiration.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: A long notice window can trap tenants into another full term.
meta.market_standard: Renewal should require affirmative consent or a reasonable 30-60 day notice window.
meta.counter: Renewal requires written agreement by both parties, or tenant may opt out with 30 days' notice before expiration.
meta.demo_hook: Miss a deadline and you owe another year.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant must provide 90 days' written move-out notice or pay two additional months of rent after vacating.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: The penalty is harsh and may surprise tenants at the end of a fixed term.
meta.market_standard: Move-out notice should be reasonable and any fee should reflect actual damages.
meta.counter: Tenant will provide 30 days' move-out notice before the lease end date, with no extra rent owed after surrender unless required by law.
meta.demo_hook: Leaving on time can still cost two months.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant must carry renter's insurance with $1,000,000 liability coverage and name landlord as additional insured.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: The coverage requirement may be higher than typical renter policies and additional insured language can be overbroad.
meta.market_standard: Reasonable renter's insurance limits are common, but requirements should be achievable and clear.
meta.counter: Tenant will maintain commercially available renter's insurance with reasonable liability limits and may name landlord as an interested party, not additional insured.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant shall pay landlord's attorney fees and costs in any dispute, regardless of outcome.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: One-way fee clauses discourage tenants from asserting valid rights.
meta.market_standard: Fee provisions should be mutual and tied to the prevailing party.
meta.counter: Attorney fees may be awarded only to the prevailing party where allowed by law, and the provision applies equally to both parties.
meta.demo_hook: You pay their lawyer even if you win.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant knowingly waives trial by jury for all claims involving the premises, landlord, property manager, or this lease.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: This can remove a procedural right tenants may not realize they are giving up.
meta.market_standard: Consumer leases often avoid broad procedural waivers or make them conspicuous and mutual.
meta.counter: Delete the jury trial waiver, or make any procedural waiver mutual, conspicuous, and subject to applicable law.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant may not withhold rent, repair and deduct, or offset any amount for landlord's failure to perform repairs.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: This may conflict with tenant remedies for habitability problems.
meta.market_standard: The lease should not waive non-waivable statutory repair or habitability remedies.
meta.counter: Nothing in this lease limits tenant's rights or remedies under applicable habitability, repair, or consumer protection laws.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant accepts the premises as-is and waives all claims concerning condition, habitability, defects, pests, mold, appliances, and building systems.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: Habitability duties often cannot be waived, and as-is language can hide serious problems.
meta.market_standard: The lease may acknowledge current condition but should preserve habitability obligations.
meta.counter: Tenant accepts visible condition at move-in, but landlord remains responsible for habitability, code compliance, and required repairs.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant acknowledges the premises may contain mold and releases landlord from all mold-related claims.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: Broad mold releases can shift health and remediation risk to the tenant.
meta.market_standard: Known conditions should be disclosed and remediation obligations should remain with the landlord when required.
meta.counter: Landlord must disclose known mold conditions and promptly remediate moisture or mold issues as required by law.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant is solely responsible for all pest control, extermination, and related treatment costs regardless of source.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: Pest issues can be building-wide or pre-existing.
meta.market_standard: Tenant handles pest issues caused by tenant conduct; landlord handles pre-existing or building-wide infestations.
meta.counter: Tenant is responsible only for pest issues caused by tenant or guests. Landlord is responsible for pre-existing, structural, or building-wide pest issues.
meta.demo_hook: Building pest problems become your bill.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant may not make alterations, paint, install fixtures, or make structural changes without landlord's prior written consent.

meta.label: standard
meta.why: Landlords commonly control physical changes to the unit.
meta.market_standard: Consent requirements for alterations are common if reasonable and not used to block minor reversible changes.
meta.counter: Tenant may make minor, non-damaging, reversible changes that are removed before move-out, subject to reasonable building rules.

clause_playbook.text: No guest may stay overnight without landlord's prior written consent, and any unauthorized guest is a material breach.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: Overnight guest bans are intrusive and hard to comply with.
meta.market_standard: Leases usually limit long-term occupancy rather than ordinary guests.
meta.counter: Tenant may have ordinary guests. Guests staying more than 14 consecutive days or 30 days in a year require landlord approval, not to be unreasonably withheld.

clause_playbook.text: Any change in occupants requires landlord approval and a $500 administrative processing fee.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: A high administrative fee may deter reasonable roommate changes.
meta.market_standard: Occupancy changes may require screening, but fees should be disclosed, reasonable, and tied to actual costs.
meta.counter: Occupant changes require reasonable approval and any screening or administrative fees must be disclosed, lawful, and limited to actual third-party costs.

clause_playbook.text: Any breach by tenant triggers liquidated damages equal to three months' rent in addition to all other remedies.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: A fixed penalty for any breach may be excessive and duplicative.
meta.market_standard: Fees should approximate actual damages and avoid stacking penalties.
meta.counter: Delete the liquidated damages clause. Landlord may recover only lawful, documented actual damages and remedies allowed by law.
meta.demo_hook: Any breach costs three months rent.

clause_playbook.text: If tenant holds over after expiration, tenant owes double rent for each day of holdover plus all consequential damages.

meta.label: standard
meta.why: Holdover rent premiums are common, though consequential damages should be reasonable and documented.
meta.market_standard: Holdover rent premiums are common when clearly disclosed and lawful.
meta.counter: Holdover rent may not exceed the maximum allowed by law, and consequential damages must be documented and reasonably foreseeable.
meta.demo_hook: Common but should be capped by law.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant acknowledges that no oral statements, promises, advertisements, listings, or representations are binding unless written in this lease.

meta.label: standard
meta.why: Integration clauses are common, but important promises should be written into the lease.
meta.market_standard: The written lease should include all important promises about repairs, concessions, parking, appliances, and amenities.
meta.counter: Add any promised repairs, concessions, parking, appliances, or amenities as written lease addenda before signing.

clause_playbook.text: Landlord is not liable for theft, fire, flood, water intrusion, utility failure, personal injury, or property damage from any cause.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: This tries to disclaim liability even for landlord negligence or building conditions.
meta.market_standard: Landlords may disclaim some personal property risk but not their own negligence or statutory duties.
meta.counter: Landlord's limitation of liability does not apply to landlord negligence, willful misconduct, breach of lease, or non-waivable legal duties.

clause_playbook.text: Parking is provided as a courtesy and may be revoked, reassigned, or charged separately at landlord's discretion.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: If parking matters to the tenant, this makes it unstable and potentially more expensive.
meta.market_standard: Parking included in rent should be identified and remain available during the term except for reasonable temporary disruptions.
meta.counter: Parking space availability, number, and price should be fixed during the lease term unless tenant agrees otherwise in writing.

clause_playbook.text: Amenities including gym, pool, laundry, storage, elevator, package room, and common areas may be closed or removed without rent adjustment.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: Material amenities can disappear even when they influenced rent.
meta.market_standard: Temporary closures are common, but permanent or extended loss of material amenities should have a remedy.
meta.counter: Temporary amenity closures are allowed for safety, repairs, or legal compliance. Extended or permanent loss of material amenities should allow a reasonable rent adjustment.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant agrees to comply with all rules now existing and any future rules landlord adopts, modifies, or revokes at any time.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: Future rules could materially change the bargain after signing.
meta.market_standard: Rules can change for safety and operations but should be reasonable, prospective, and not materially alter rent or core rights.
meta.counter: Future rules must be reasonable, prospective, uniformly applied, and may not materially increase tenant obligations or reduce lease rights.

clause_playbook.text: Any smoke, odor, candle, incense, or vapor condition may result in cleaning, repainting, carpet replacement, and odor remediation fees at landlord's discretion.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: The trigger is broad and fees are discretionary.
meta.market_standard: No-smoking rules are common, but charges should require actual damage and itemization.
meta.counter: Charges apply only for documented smoke-related damage beyond ordinary wear, supported by itemized invoices.

clause_playbook.text: Landlord may approve pets subject to pet rent, pet deposit, pet fees, DNA registration, cleaning charges, and additional rules set at any time.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: Open-ended pet costs can increase after move-in.
meta.market_standard: Pet fees and rules should be disclosed before signing and remain stable during the term.
meta.counter: All pet deposits, rent, fees, and rules must be listed in the lease and may not increase during the lease term without tenant consent.

clause_playbook.text: All animals, including assistance, emotional support, or service animals, require pet rent and nonrefundable pet fees.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: Assistance animal charges may violate fair housing rules.
meta.market_standard: Assistance animals should be handled under applicable fair housing accommodation rules, not ordinary pet fee rules.
meta.counter: Assistance animals approved as reasonable accommodations are not subject to pet rent, pet deposits, or pet fees except as allowed by law.
meta.demo_hook: Service animal charged like a pet.

clause_playbook.text: The security deposit is nonrefundable and may be applied by landlord in landlord's sole discretion.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: Calling a security deposit nonrefundable is often improper and undermines itemization protections.
meta.market_standard: Deposits should be refundable except for lawful, itemized deductions.
meta.counter: Security deposit is refundable except for lawful, itemized deductions for unpaid rent, damage beyond ordinary wear, or other charges allowed by law.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant must pay $250 for any lost key, fob, lockout, mailbox key, remote, or access device.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: A flat fee may exceed actual replacement or lockout costs.
meta.market_standard: Replacement fees should be reasonable and tied to actual costs.
meta.counter: Replacement and lockout fees must be reasonable, disclosed, and limited to actual replacement, vendor, or locksmith costs.

clause_playbook.text: All notices to tenant may be sent by email, text, portal, or posting and are deemed received immediately upon sending.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: Immediate deemed receipt can be unfair if the tenant misses an electronic notice.
meta.market_standard: Notice methods and receipt timing should match applicable law and provide a reasonable delivery period.
meta.counter: Notices are effective only when delivered according to applicable law, with email or portal notices deemed received after a reasonable period unless confirmed earlier.

clause_playbook.text: Landlord may relocate tenant to another unit at any time for any reason upon 5 days' notice.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: This undermines the tenant's right to the specific unit they rented.
meta.market_standard: Relocation should be limited to emergencies or mutually agreed substitutions.
meta.counter: Tenant may be relocated only for emergency, legal, or habitability reasons, to a comparable or better unit, with reasonable notice and landlord-paid moving costs.
meta.demo_hook: You can be moved with 5 days' notice.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant waives claims and rent reduction rights for construction, noise, dust, renovation, interruption, or loss of access caused by landlord or neighboring work.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: Extended construction can materially affect living conditions.
meta.market_standard: Temporary disruptions are expected, but substantial interference should preserve legal remedies.
meta.counter: Tenant does not waive remedies for substantial interference with quiet enjoyment, habitability, access, or services caused by landlord-controlled work.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant is responsible for all bedbug inspection, treatment, relocation, laundry, disposal, and extermination costs.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: Bedbug responsibility often depends on source, notice, and building conditions.
meta.market_standard: Treatment responsibility should follow applicable law and fault, not automatically fall on the tenant.
meta.counter: Bedbug inspection and treatment responsibilities will follow applicable law and documented cause. Tenant is responsible only where tenant caused the infestation.
meta.demo_hook: All bedbug costs pushed to tenant.

clause_playbook.text: Appliances are provided for tenant convenience only and landlord has no duty to repair or replace them.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: If appliances are part of the unit, tenants expect them to work.
meta.market_standard: Included appliances should be maintained unless explicitly excluded before rent is set.
meta.counter: Landlord will maintain and repair appliances provided with the premises unless damage is caused by tenant misuse.
meta.demo_hook: Included appliances are not really included.

clause_playbook.text: Landlord is not responsible for interruption of water, heat, gas, electricity, internet, elevator, trash, or other services for any reason.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: Essential service interruptions can create habitability issues.
meta.market_standard: Landlord should not disclaim responsibility for essential services within landlord's control.
meta.counter: Landlord remains responsible for essential services and building systems within landlord's control, subject to applicable habitability laws.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant will pay a mandatory move-out cleaning fee of $600 regardless of unit condition.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: Mandatory flat cleaning fees can duplicate deposit deductions.
meta.market_standard: Cleaning charges should reflect actual condition beyond ordinary wear and be disclosed clearly.
meta.counter: Move-out cleaning charges apply only if the unit is not returned in the condition required by the lease, ordinary wear excepted, and must be itemized.
meta.demo_hook: Cleaning fee applies even if spotless.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant must promptly notify landlord of needed repairs. Landlord will make repairs required by law within a reasonable time after notice.

meta.label: standard
meta.why: This creates clear repair notice duties for tenant and response duties for landlord.
meta.market_standard: Prompt repair notice and reasonable landlord response are standard.
meta.counter: No change needed.

clause_playbook.text: Tenant will pay monthly rent of the stated amount on the first day of each month during the lease term.

meta.label: standard
meta.why: A fixed rent amount and due date are expected in a lease.
meta.market_standard: Rent should be clearly stated and fixed for the term.
meta.counter: No change needed.

clause_playbook.text: Your option will vest over four years, with 25% vesting after one year of service and the remainder vesting monthly thereafter.

meta.label: standard
meta.why: Four-year vesting with a one-year cliff is common for startup employees.
meta.market_standard: Four-year vesting with a one-year cliff and monthly vesting after the cliff.
meta.counter: No change needed unless negotiating for acceleration or credit for prior service.

clause_playbook.text: No vesting acceleration will apply upon a change in control, termination, or any other event.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: No acceleration can leave the employee exposed if the company is sold or the role is eliminated.
meta.market_standard: Senior hires often ask for single-trigger or double-trigger acceleration, especially after an acquisition.
meta.counter: Add double-trigger acceleration for 50% to 100% of unvested equity if employment is terminated without cause or for good reason within 12 months after a change in control.
meta.demo_hook: No protection if the company sells.

clause_playbook.text: Vested options must be exercised within 30 days after termination of employment.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: A short exercise window can force the employee to spend cash quickly or lose vested equity.
meta.market_standard: Many candidates negotiate an extended post-termination exercise window.
meta.counter: Extend the post-termination exercise window for vested options to at least 12 months, and preferably the full option term where permitted.
meta.demo_hook: You can lose vested equity after 30 days.

clause_playbook.text: Employee assigns to company all inventions, discoveries, works, ideas, code, designs, and intellectual property created before, during, or after employment that relate in any way to company business.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: This can capture pre-existing side projects and future work outside the job.
meta.market_standard: IP assignment should be limited to work created during employment using company resources or related to assigned duties.
meta.counter: Limit assignment to IP created during employment that relates to company business or uses company resources, and list excluded prior inventions in an exhibit.
meta.demo_hook: Your side project may become theirs.

clause_playbook.text: For 12 months after employment, employee may not work for, advise, invest in, or assist any business that competes with company.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: Broad non-competes can limit future employment and may be unenforceable or restricted in some states.
meta.market_standard: Restrictive covenants should be narrow, lawful, and focused on confidential information and non-solicitation where permitted.
meta.counter: Delete the non-compete. Replace with a confidentiality covenant and any lawful, narrowly tailored non-solicitation obligations.
meta.demo_hook: This can block your next job.

clause_playbook.text: Employment is at will and may be terminated by either you or the company at any time, with or without cause or notice.

meta.label: standard
meta.why: At-will employment is standard in many US startup offer letters.
meta.market_standard: At-will language is common, though executives may negotiate severance or notice.
meta.counter: No change needed for most roles; senior candidates may ask for severance if terminated without cause.

clause_playbook.text: You are not entitled to severance, continued benefits, acceleration, or other payments upon termination.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: For senior or hard-to-replace roles, no severance may be worth negotiating.
meta.market_standard: Senior hires often ask for 1-3 months of severance and benefits continuation for termination without cause.
meta.counter: Add severance equal to 8 weeks of base salary and benefits continuation if terminated without cause, subject to a standard release.

clause_playbook.text: Any bonus is fully discretionary and may be modified, reduced, or eliminated at any time.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: A discretionary-only bonus may not be meaningful if it was part of the compensation pitch.
meta.market_standard: Target bonuses should identify target amount, criteria, and eligibility timing.
meta.counter: State the target bonus amount, eligibility period, payment timing, and any objective performance criteria.

clause_playbook.text: If you leave or are terminated for any reason within 24 months, you must repay all relocation expenses and signing bonus amounts.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: Repayment after involuntary termination is harsh and 24 months can be long.
meta.market_standard: Repayment obligations are usually prorated and often excluded for termination without cause.
meta.counter: Repayment should be prorated monthly and waived if employment ends due to termination without cause, layoff, disability, death, or company breach.
meta.demo_hook: You owe money if they lay you off.

clause_playbook.text: Employee must keep confidential all information learned during employment, whether or not public, known, or independently developed.

meta.label: red-flag
meta.why: Confidentiality should not cover public information or knowledge independently developed outside the company.
meta.market_standard: Confidentiality obligations include standard carveouts for public information, prior knowledge, and independent development.
meta.counter: Add carveouts for information that is public, already known without duty of confidentiality, independently developed, or lawfully received from another source.

clause_playbook.text: Employee may not engage in outside employment, consulting, advisory work, open-source work, or business activity without company approval.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: This can block harmless side projects, advising, or open-source contributions.
meta.market_standard: Outside activity limits should focus on conflicts of interest, use of company resources, and interference with job duties.
meta.counter: Permit outside activities that do not compete with company, use company confidential information or resources, or interfere with job duties.

clause_playbook.text: The equity grant is subject to approval by the board of directors and the terms of the company's equity plan.

meta.label: standard
meta.why: Board approval is common, but candidates should ask for the grant size and plan documents.
meta.market_standard: Offer letters should state the number of shares or options, exercise price methodology, vesting, and approval process.
meta.counter: Confirm the number of shares/options, fully diluted ownership percentage, vesting schedule, exercise price methodology, and plan documents.

clause_playbook.text: You will receive an option to purchase 50,000 shares of common stock.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: Share count alone does not tell the candidate ownership percentage or dilution context.
meta.market_standard: Candidates should understand the fully diluted percentage and latest valuation context.
meta.counter: Provide the approximate fully diluted ownership percentage represented by the grant and current total shares outstanding.

clause_playbook.text: Employee agrees to binding arbitration for all employment-related claims and waives class, collective, and representative actions.

meta.label: unusual
meta.why: Arbitration and class waivers can limit procedural rights and shared claims.
meta.market_standard: Dispute terms should comply with applicable law and preserve non-waivable rights.
meta.counter: Confirm the clause preserves all non-waivable rights and uses a neutral arbitration provider with company-paid fees where required.

clause_playbook.text: This offer is contingent on satisfactory completion of background checks, employment eligibility verification, and execution of company agreements.

meta.label: standard
meta.why: Contingencies are common, but candidates should review all required agreements before resigning from a current job.
meta.market_standard: Contingencies should be clear and all required agreements should be provided before acceptance.
meta.counter: Ask for all required agreements and policies before accepting and before resigning from any current role.

user_contracts.text: Landlord leases to Tenant the residential unit located at 123 Maple Street, Unit 4B, San Francisco, CA, for residential use only.
The lease begins on July 1, 2026 and ends on June 30, 2027. Renewal requires written agreement by both parties.
Tenant will pay monthly rent of $2,800 on the first day of each month during the lease term.

Tenant will pay a refundable security deposit of $2,800. Deductions are limited to unpaid rent, damage beyond ordinary wear and tear, and other lawful charges. Landlord will provide an itemized statement and return any remaining deposit within the deadline required by applicable law.
If rent is not received within 5 days after the due date, Tenant will pay a late fee of $50 or the maximum allowed by law, whichever is less.

Tenant must promptly notify Landlord of needed repairs. Landlord will maintain the premises, building systems, included appliances, and habitability conditions as required by law. Tenant is responsible for damage caused by Tenant or guests.
Landlord may enter the premises with at least 24 hours' notice during reasonable hours for inspections, repairs, or showings, except in emergencies or as otherwise allowed by law.
Tenant is responsible for electricity and internet. Landlord is responsible for water, trash, and common-area utilities.
Tenant may have ordinary guests. Guests staying more than 14 consecutive days require Landlord approval, not to be unreasonably withheld.
Tenant may request assignment or sublet approval. Landlord consent may not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed.
Tenant will return the premises in substantially the same condition as received, ordinary wear and tear excepted.
This lease and written addenda are the full agreement between the parties.
Landlord leases to Tenant the residential unit located at 500 Valencia Street, Unit 9, San Francisco, CA.
Tenant will pay monthly rent of $2,950 on the first day of each month during the lease term.

The security deposit is nonrefundable and may be applied by Landlord in Landlord's sole discretion. Landlord may retain deposit amounts for general wear and tear, odor, paint touch-up, carpet cleaning, or administrative review.
Tenant waives claims and rent reduction rights for construction, noise, dust, renovation, interruption, or loss of access caused by Landlord or neighboring work.
Landlord may relocate Tenant to another unit at any time for any reason upon 5 days' notice.
Landlord is not responsible for interruption of water, heat, gas, electricity, internet, elevator, trash, or other services for any reason.
Tenant must provide 90 days' written move-out notice or pay two additional months of rent after vacating.
Tenant shall pay Landlord's attorney fees and costs in any dispute, regardless of outcome.
Tenant agrees to comply with all rules now existing and any future rules Landlord adopts, modifies, or revokes at any time.

Tenant acknowledges the premises may contain mold and releases Landlord from all mold-related claims. Tenant is responsible for all bedbug inspection, treatment, relocation, laundry, disposal, and extermination costs.
Appliances are provided for Tenant convenience only and Landlord has no duty to repair or replace them.
All notices to Tenant may be sent by email, text, portal, or posting and are deemed received immediately upon sending.
Landlord leases to Tenant the apartment located at 88 Mission Alley, Unit 12, San Francisco, CA.
The lease begins on July 1, 2026 and ends on June 30, 2027. This lease automatically renews for another 12 months unless Tenant gives written notice at least 120 days before expiration.
Tenant will pay monthly rent of $3,100 on the first day of each month. Landlord may increase rent, fees, utility charges, or other amounts at any time upon written notice.

Tenant will pay a security deposit of $3,100. Security deposit will be returned after Landlord completes inspection and accounting. Landlord may retain all or part of the security deposit for cleaning, repainting, carpet wear, general wear and tear, administrative costs, or any other condition Landlord deems necessary.
If rent is late, Tenant will pay a late fee of $150 plus $25 per day until rent is paid in full.
Tenant is responsible for all repairs, replacements, maintenance, pest control, appliance failures, plumbing issues, electrical issues, and habitability-related expenses during the lease term.
Landlord may enter the premises at any time for inspection, repairs, showings, maintenance, emergencies, or any business purpose.
Tenant agrees to pay all utilities, shared charges, building charges, service fees, billing fees, and any other charges allocated by Landlord.
No guest may stay overnight without Landlord's prior written consent, and any unauthorized guest is a material breach.
If Tenant terminates early, Tenant remains liable for all rent through the end of the lease term, regardless of whether Landlord re-rents the premises.
Tenant shall indemnify Landlord from all claims, losses, damages, costs, and attorney fees arising from the premises, regardless of cause.
Tenant waives the right to bring or participate in any class action and agrees all disputes must be resolved by binding arbitration selected by Landlord.
You are offered the position of Founding Product Engineer, reporting to the CEO. Employment is at will and may be terminated by either you or the company at any time, with or without cause or notice.
Your base salary will be $155,000 per year, paid according to the company's normal payroll schedule. Any bonus is fully discretionary and may be modified, reduced, or eliminated at any time.

Subject to board approval, you will receive an option to purchase 50,000 shares of common stock. Your option will vest over four years, with 25% vesting after one year of service and the remainder vesting monthly thereafter. No vesting acceleration will apply upon a change in control, termination, or any other event.
Vested options must be exercised within 30 days after termination of employment.
Employee assigns to company all inventions, discoveries, works, ideas, code, designs, and intellectual property created before, during, or after employment that relate in any way to company business.
Employee may not engage in outside employment, consulting, advisory work, open-source work, or business activity without company approval.
For 12 months after employment, employee may not work for, advise, invest in, or assist any business that competes with company.
You are not entitled to severance, continued benefits, acceleration, or other payments upon termination.
This offer is contingent on satisfactory completion of background checks, employment eligibility verification, and execution of company agreements.
